subject: toxicology
title: Organic solvent toxicity
preambleMarkdown:
# Organic solvent toxicity

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Organic solvent toxicity is a medication or pharmacology topic in toxicology.
- This article focuses on exposure, dose, timing, toxidrome, decontamination, antidote, and delayed toxicity.
- The practical clinical priority is therapeutic benefit balanced against toxicity and interactions.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Organic solvent toxicity to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Organic solvent toxicity is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is therapeutic benefit balanced against toxicity and interactions.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Drug class and mechanism.
- Therapeutic indication and line of therapy.
- Route, formulation, onset, duration, metabolism, and elimination.
- Expected adverse effects, dangerous toxicities, interactions, and special-population precautions.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Organic solvent toxicity depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In toxicology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Organic solvent toxicity.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Organic solvent toxicity disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Organic solvent toxicity should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Organic solvent toxicity first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Organic solvent toxicity, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Patients may present with the condition being treated, lack of response, adverse effects, overdose, withdrawal, or interaction.
- Important history includes dose, timing, adherence, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status, allergies, alcohol or substance use, and other medications.
- Medication harms may mimic disease progression, so compare symptom timing with drug exposure.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Organic solvent toxicity, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused toxicology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Organic solvent toxicity presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Organic solvent toxicity with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Organic solvent toxicity is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Organic solvent toxicity should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm indication, contraindications, dose, route, duration, and monitoring before starting or continuing therapy.
- Adjust or avoid therapy for renal impairment, hepatic impairment, pregnancy, lactation, age, allergy, and interactions.
- Manage toxicity by stopping exposure, supporting physiology, using antidotes or reversal when indicated, and monitoring delayed effects.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Organic solvent toxicity is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses therapeutic benefit balanced against toxicity and interactions.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Choose therapy by mechanism, target condition, expected benefit, toxicity risk, interactions, and monitoring burden.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use drug levels, ECG monitoring, decontamination, dialysis, reversal, or antidote pathways only when they change management.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Organic solvent toxicity, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Organic solvent toxicity may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Organic solvent toxicity patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Organic solvent toxicity, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Organic solvent toxicity in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Organic solvent toxicity, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Organic solvent toxicity does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Organic solvent toxicity may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Organic solvent toxicity, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Organic solvent toxicity as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know mechanism, main use, major contraindication, dangerous toxicity, monitoring test, and key interaction.
- Renal and hepatic function often decide dose safety.
- A medication list is part of the diagnostic workup, not an administrative detail.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Organic solvent toxicity draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

### Source Excerpt: Industrial Chemicals

_Source lines: 38221-38495._

```text
VIII. INDUSTRIAL CHEMICALS
A. Organophosphates and carbamates are cholinesterase-inhibiting insecticides,
available for use in both industrial and home settings.

## 1. Organophosphates

a. Discussion. Organophosphates are carbon-containing molecules derived
from phosphorous acid. The phosphate group is responsible for the mol-
ecule's binding and toxicity.

i. These agents are absorbed through dermal, conjunctival, gastrointestinal,
and pulmonary surfaces. Symptoms occur minutes to hours post expo-
sure. Fat-soluble organophosphates may accumulate in fat, producing
delayed or prolonged symptoms.

ii. The organophosphate forms a complex with acetylcholinesterase. Without
treatment, the enzyme is "aged" and destroyed, preventing the breakdown
of acetylcholine. There is a wide variability in an individual's "normal" ace-
tylcholinesterase activity. Generally, symptoms occur when the acetylcholin-
esterase activity is less than 50% of baseline, indicative of acute poisoning.

iii. Acetylcholine accumulates, stimulates, and exhausts cholinergic recep-
tors. Cholinergic excess occurs in the parasympathetic system, in the
preganglionic synapses of the sympathetic system, and at the neuromus-
cular junction. Sites of toxicity include:
a) Muscarinic sites (i.e., postganglionic parasympathetic synapses)
b) Nicotinic sites (i.e., autonomic ganglia, somatic neuromuscular endplates)
c) CNS sites
b. Clinical features. Patients often exhibit a garlic-like or petroleum-like odor.

NCPE, hyperthermia, and hepatotoxicity may also occur (see Clinical Pearl 20-7).

c. Differential diagnoses
i. Ingestion of or exposure to carbamates or short-acting cholinesterase in-
hibitors (e.g., pyridostigmine and some ophthalmic preparations used to treat
glaucoma, such as demecarium and echothiophate) should be considered.

ii. CNS depressant ingestions, head injury, stroke, pulmonary edema,
sepsis, and severe gastroenteritis may be ruled out.

d. Evaluation
i. General studies. An ABG determination should be made, and the need
for mechanical ventilation assessed. A glucose level, complete blood
count, electrolyte panel, BUN and creatinine levels, and liver function
tests should be ordered. The ECG should be assessed for dysrhythmias.

ii. Confirmatory studies
a) Erythrocyte (true) cholinesterase is found in nervous tissue and on
erythrocytes. This level most accurately reflects cholinesterase activ-
ity in nervous tissues.

b) Plasma (pseudo) cholinesterase is produced in the liver and circulated
in the plasma. This level is more labile and less specific than the true
cholinesterase level and is affected by liver disease, cirrhosis, malnutri-
tion, chronic inflammation, and morphine or codeine administration.

Quick 	HIT
Toxicity is graded according
to the percentage of ace-
tylcholinesterase activity:
mild toxicity & 20% to 50%,
moderate toxicity & 10% to
20%, and severe toxicity &
less than 10%.

Other signs and symptoms of organophosphate intoxication are classified as:
1. Muscarinic (e.g., salivation, lacrimation, urination, defecation, gastrointestinal cramping, emesis, bron-
chospasm, bronchorrhea, sweating, and miosis)
2. Nicotinic (e.g., muscle fasciculations, cramping, weakness, paralysis, areflexia, tachycardia, hyperten-
sion, pallor, and hyperglycemia)
3. CNS (e.g., restlessness, agitation, headache, drowsiness, confusion, ataxia, delirium, seizures, and coma)
PEARL	CLINICAL 	20-7
T O X I C O L O G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	467

Toxicologic Emergencies
e. Therapy
i. Initial stabilization involves airway management (including endotracheal in-
tubation, mechanical ventilation, vigorous suctioning, and the administration
of supplemental oxygen as necessary), cardiovascular resuscitation, and treat-
ment of ventricular arrhythmias. Seizures are treated with benzodiazepines.

ii. Decontamination
a) Gastric decontamination. Gastric lavage may be indicated if the
patient presents early after ingestion. Activated charcoal should be
administered in most cases.

b) Dermal decontamination. Contaminated clothing should be removed
and placed in plastic bags for disposal. Skin, hair, and nails should be
washed with soap and water, and the conjunctivae should be irrigated.

1) Wash water and irrigation fluids must be disposed of separately.

2) Health care workers must wear protective clothing, masks, and
rubber gloves to prevent secondary exposure and toxicity.

iii. Antidote treatment
a) Atropine antagonizes the effects of acetylcholine. It reverses mus-
carinic effects and penetrates the CNS to alleviate effects at central
cholinergic receptors.

1) Indications. Atropine should be administered as soon as evidence
of cholinergic excess is apparent. Tachycardia is not a contrain-
dication to atropine administration. Heart rate may actually de-
crease as bronchial secretions dry and oxygenation improves.

2) Dosage. The initial dose in adults is 2 to 4 mg intravenously, and
in children, 0.05 mg/kg intravenously. This dose may be doubled
every 5 to 10 minutes.

b) Pralidoxime (2-PAM) attacks the phosphorylated acetylcholinester-
ase, freeing the enzyme. It acts at nicotinic and muscarinic sites and
may relieve CNS toxicity.

1) Indications. Pralidoxime should be administered as soon as evi-
dence of cholinergic excess is apparent or if organophosphate
poisoning is suspected, preferably within 24 hours. Late presenta-
tion does not preclude its use.

2) Dosage. The adult dose is 1 g administered intravenously (20 to
40 mg/kg, up to a total dose of 2 g in children). This dose may be
repeated or continuous infusion may be used.

f. Disposition
i. Discharge. Patients with inconsequential exposures who have been
monitored for 6 to 8 hours and who have no cholinergic symptoms may
be discharged.

ii. Admission. Patients who are symptomatic or who have received atro-
pine, pralidoxime, or both should be admitted to an ICU. Patients with
ingestions or exposures to fat-soluble organophosphates should be ad-
mitted and monitored for delayed onset of toxicity.

## 2. Carbamates

a. Discussion. Carbamate insecticides cause cholinergic symptoms, but CNS
toxicity is thought to be minimal because these chemicals do not penetrate
the blood-brain barrier to a great extent.

i. Carbamates are readily absorbed after dermal, gastrointestinal, and pul-
monary exposure.

ii. Carbamates prevent the normal breakdown of acetylcholine, causing
cholinergic excess. However, their bond to acetylcholinesterase sponta-
neously hydrolyzes, and therefore symptoms are usually less severe and
less prolonged (as compared with those of organophosphate toxicity).

b. Clinical features: Carbamate-induced cholinergic toxicity consists of mus-
carinic and nicotinic symptoms, but CNS toxicity is generally less than that
of organophosphates.

c. Differential diagnoses include ingestion of organophosphates or other
cholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., physostigmine, pyridostigmine), CNS
Quick 	HIT
The endpoint for atropine
administration is the drying
of bronchial secretions.

468 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Toxicologic Emergencies
depressant ingestion, head injury, stroke, pulmonary edema, cardiogenic
shock, sepsis, and severe gastroenteritis.

d. Evaluation should proceed as for organophosphates (see VIII.A.1.d). True
and pseudocholinesterase levels should be obtained.

e. Therapy entails airway management, stabilization, and decontamination.

Antidote treatment is with atropine. The administration of pralidoxime in
cases of carbamate poisoning is controversial but may improve respiratory
function and may also be administered.

f. Disposition
i. Discharge. Patients with inconsequential exposures who are monitored
for 6 hours and show no signs of cholinergic excess may be discharged.

ii. Admission. Symptomatic patients and patients who have been treated
with atropine, pralidoxime, or both should be admitted to an ICU.

B. Hydrocarbons are a diverse group of organic compounds with a wide range of
toxicity. In addition, hydrocarbons are often used as a solvent for other chemicals,
which can have their own intrinsic toxicity.

## 1. Discussion

a. Incidence. Children younger than 5 years have the highest incidence of
reported exposure requiring hospital admission.

b. Pathophysiology. Toxicity occurs mainly in three organ systems:
i. Lung toxicity is caused by aspiration and leads to respiratory fail-
ure. Aspiration is most likely with hydrocarbons of low viscosity
and high volatility. The lungs are the organ most often affected in
acute exposures.

ii. Cardiac toxicity produces dysrhythmias.

iii. CNS toxicity produces lethargy, stupor, coma, and seizures.

2. Clinical features
a. Patient history. A history of coughing, grunting, gasping, or vomiting
strongly suggests aspiration.

b. Physical examination findings may include tachypnea, stridor, cyanosis,
rales, respiratory distress, irregular heart rate, and CNS depression.

i. CNS and cardiac signs usually occur within 1 hour of intoxication.

ii. Pulmonary signs and symptoms usually become apparent later.

3. Differential diagnoses include child abuse, salicylate toxicity, caustic inges-
tion, cardiogenic pulmonary edema, or secondary poisonings (e.g., from pes-
ticides containing hydrocarbons as a solvent).

4. Evaluation is with continuous cardiac monitoring and pulse oximetry. An ABG
determination, electrolyte panel, and chest radiograph may also be considered.

5. Therapy. Aggressive supportive care is the mainstay of treatment.

a. Initial stabilization is according to ACLS protocols. Cardiopulmonary re-
suscitation may be necessary.

i. In cases of severe respiratory distress and persistent hypoxia, consider
intubation, positive end-expiratory pressure, and extracorporeal mem-
brane oxygenation.

ii. Epinephrine should be avoided because of the theoretical risk of cate-
cholamine sensitization of the heart, leading to ventricular dysrhythmias.

b. Gastric decontamination is a relative contraindication because decontami-
nation procedures may increase the chance of aspiration. Decontamination
should be considered in cases of toxic co-ingestions or when hydrocarbons
have been ingested in association with camphor, heavy metals, toluene, ben-
zene, pesticides, carbon tetrachloride, methylene chloride, or other haloge-
nated hydrocarbons.

## 6. Disposition

a. Discharge. Patients who are asymptomatic after 4 to 6 hours of observa-
tion and who have normal physical examination findings, normal results on
room air ABG determinations or pulse oximetry, and a normal chest radio-
graph may be discharged home with a responsible party.

b. Admission. Symptomatic patients must be admitted. Patients who have sig-
nificant persistent hypoxia should be admitted to an ICU.

Quick 	HIT
There are no specific an-
tidotes for hydrocarbon
ingestion, however, always
consider the possibility
of co-ingestion.

T O X I C O L O G I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	469

ENVIRONMENTAL
EMERGENCIES
I. INTRODUCTION
This chapter deals with the diagnosis and treatment of illness and injury related to
nature's dangerous forces, including wildlife. Also discussed in this chapter are the
special problems encountered when the sick or injured patient is located far from
civilization-the subspecialty of wilderness medicine.
```
